---
title: "Stave Churches in Norway"
description: "A Sight for Cold Eyes"
author: "Sophia Jaffer"
date: "2026-06-17"
image: "stave churches.jpeg"
categories:
  - Norway
  - Architecture
---

## Stave Churches in Norway

![Stave Church](stave churches 2.jpg)

In January of 2024, I travelled to Norway to stay at my friend’s cabin, and ski for the first time. We met while she was studying abroad in London and have since taken turns to show each other some of the dear and wonderful parts of our respective countries. We stayed a few hours from Oslo, and drove back in the chilling winter snow, my toes perpetually numb, every moment outside marked by the feeling of frost coating the inside of my nostrils. We saw great stone trolls, sweeping snowy roads, dense fir forests, and through the blanket of white, the unique, awesome spires of Norway’s surviving stave churches. Only 28 stave churches remain standing in Norway, constructed from wood, largely built between 1150 and 1350. They are common in high valleys and forest land, where the areas are less populated. While hundreds of stave churches were originally built, the majority have been destroyed through redesign, conversion, dismantlement or natural disasters like fires, storms or avalanches.

We couldn’t stay outside for long. A true Brit, even my warmest clothes were unequipped for the Norway cold. However, these great and beautiful buildings stick in my mind. I am always fond of a spire, tied as I am to the welcoming beacon of home that is the spire of Salisbury cathedral, the rich maroon against the stark white snow, the peaking headstones, the elegant and neat wood. On the same drive, we saw a moose. I have never been to Vermont, but I listen to Noah Kahan every time I set food in Norway, and when I get home, I’m always Homesick for a country that’s not my own.
